title: NIS2 OT Network Segmentation Validation
exclusions: Does not validate physical access controls, safety instrumented system programming integrity, or vendor remote-access contracts; it only sees what the paired firewall and IDS logs chose to record and forward during the sampled interval.
evidence: Hourly `UC-22.2.36` panel snapshots, exported CSV of offending `src_zone`→`dest_zone` pairs, and signed policy lookup hash archived under `index=audit_evidence` for OT governance reviews.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(a) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) (Risk analysis and information-system security policies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.36: NIS2 OT Network Segmentation Validation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.36' running on Firewall and IDS logs tagged with zone, nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a sandbox, generate at least eleven allowed-session records from CORP-LAN toward PLC-AREA with the lookup marking that pair `allowed=false`; the search ranks the pair with `sessions>10` and non-zero `bytes`, matching the firewall’s own session counter.
controlTest.negativeScenario: When only approved maintenance pairs exist—with temporary `allowed=true` rows current in `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` and no other crossings—the hourly alert returns zero results and the empty output is stamped into the quarterly OT segmentation attestation workbook.

dataSources: `index=firewall` or `index=ot_network` with `sourcetype=pan:traffic` (src_zone, dest_zone, bytes, dest_port, app per Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks, Splunkbase 2757) and `sourcetype=cisco:asa` from Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620) with normalized zone tags; optional Zeek or IDS for corroboration. Policy lookup `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` includes src_zone, dest_zone, allowed, protocol, port. Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) enriches ownership.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757), Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620)
spl:
index=netfw earliest=-24h src_zone=* dest_zone=*
| lookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT allowed protocol port
| where allowed="false"
| stats sum(bytes) as bytes count as sessions by src_zone dest_zone dest_port app
| where sessions > 10
| sort - bytes
| table src_zone, dest_zone, dest_port, app, bytes, sessions
description: Aggregates firewall sessions that cross Purdue-style zone pairs your policy marks as forbidden—surfacing repeated SMB, RDP, or HTTP crossings from IT VLANs toward OT cells where the lookup says those conversations should never succeed in bulk.
value: Plant safety and production uptime depend on hard boundaries; this gives OT governance teams repeatable evidence that the choke-point firewalls actually see—and log—the east-west attempts that bypass paper diagrams, so regulators trust the segmentation story.
implementation: (1) Normalize `src_zone`/`dest_zone` naming between Palo Alto (`pan:traffic`) and Cisco ASA extractions. (2) Maintain `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` rows for each allowed/blocked pair with `allowed=true|false`. (3) Heavy forwarders parse locally; avoid sending raw pcaps to Splunk. (4) Save alert `UC-22.2.36` hourly; route to OT SOC distribution list. (5) Weekly workstation with plant engineering to clear false positives via exception register updates.
visualization: Sankey (zone to zone), Table (violations), Single value (distinct forbidden paths), Heatmap (sessions by hour).
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on the Network Traffic data model for CIM-based queries. Set summary range to 30 days. The accelerated variant is 10-20x faster than raw event queries for large firewall deployments.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives:
**Jump host patch management** — Distinguish: single admin IP, scheduled `SCCM` window, `app` tagged microsoft-ds. Suppress: `lookup ot_jump_hosts.csv src OUTPUT ok_jump` then `where ok_jump!=1`.

**Mis-tagged zones after firewall swap** — Distinguish: sudden flood of “violations” right after rule push; `dest_zone` equals `any`. Suppress: time-bound exception for **48 hours** tied to change ticket; validate tags via `lookup zone_mapping_audit.csv`.

**Vendor remote support VPN** — Distinguish: known supplier ASNs, small `sessions` count bursts. Suppress: extend policy CSV `allowed=true` row with protocol `tcp/443` plus comment field logged to exception register.

**Duplicate logging** — Distinguish: same flow logged by ASA and Palo Alto; double `bytes`. Suppress: dedupe using `stats sum(bytes) by flow_id` if both devices emit `session_id`; else filter with `where sourcetype=pan:traffic` only.

**PLC firmware multicast** — Distinguish: broadcast helper crossing zones but policy should allow UDP/2222. Suppress: add explicit `port` and `protocol` tuple to `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` with `allowed=true` after OT sign-off.
equipmentModels: Cisco ASA 5500-X / Firepower Threat Defense (syslog with zone and interface tags)
Palo Alto Networks PA-series firewalls (PAN-OS 10.x+ syslog with zone logging)
Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620, cisco:asa field extractions)
Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757, pan:traffic field extractions)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (asset and identity framework, notable routing)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0 recommended for asset-identity enrichment and notable routing. Not strictly required — the core SPL works without ES — but ES provides the OT SOC queue and the adaptive response framework for automated ticket creation.
• Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757) ≥7.0 installed on Search Heads and Indexers. This TA extracts `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `bytes`, `dest_port`, `app`, `action`, `rule`, `serial_number`, and `threat_id` from `sourcetype=pan:traffic`. Verify installation: `| rest /services/apps/local | search title=Splunk_TA_paloalto | table title version`.
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620) ≥4.0 installed on Search Heads and Indexers if Cisco firewalls are also deployed in the OT perimeter. Extracts `src_zone`, `dest_zone` (mapped from ASA `src_interface`/`dest_interface` names), `bytes`, `dest_port`, `transport`, `action`. Note: Cisco ASA uses interface names (e.g., `inside`, `outside`, `dmz`, `ot-cell-1`) rather than explicit zone objects — the TA maps these to `src_zone`/`dest_zone` via `transforms.conf` regex extractions.
• Firewalls: Palo Alto PA-series and/or Cisco ASA/FTD deployed as inter-zone firewalls between Purdue model levels. Each firewall must have zone-based logging enabled:
  - **Palo Alto**: Device > Log Settings > Traffic > ensure "Log at Session End" is enabled for all inter-zone security rules. Each security-policy rule must reference named zones (e.g., `CORP-LAN`, `OT-DMZ`, `PLC-CELL-1`, `SIS-ZONE`). Confirm in the PAN-OS CLI: `show log traffic direction equal backward zone.src equal CORP-LAN zone.dst equal PLC-CELL-1 last 5`.
  - **Cisco ASA**: logging must be set to at least `logging trap informational` with `logging enable`. Interface-to-zone mapping is done in the TA's `transforms.conf` — edit `TA-cisco-asa/local/transforms.conf` to map interface names to Purdue-style zone names if the default ASA interface names are not descriptive.
• Syslog forwarding: firewalls forward syslog to the Heavy Forwarder on the plant DMZ. Recommended: use SC4S (Splunk Connect for Syslog) on the HF for reliable syslog ingestion with proper source-type classification. Palo Alto uses `UDP/514` by default but supports `TLS/6514` — use TLS in OT environments. Cisco ASA uses `UDP/514`.
• CSV `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` with columns: `src_zone` (firewall zone name of the source, e.g., `CORP-LAN`), `dest_zone` (destination zone, e.g., `PLC-CELL-1`), `allowed` (`true` or `false` — whether this zone pair should ever have traffic), `protocol` (optional — `tcp`, `udp`, or `*` for any), `port` (optional — specific port like `502` for Modbus, or `*` for any), `justification` (text describing why the pair is allowed/blocked), `last_reviewed` (ISO 8601 date). This CSV encodes the operator's OT network segmentation policy derived from the Purdue/ISA-62443 zone model. Each row represents a zone-pair decision approved by the OT security governance board.
• Index `firewall` or `ot_network` with retention ≥ 90 days for the query lookback. Firewall traffic logs are high-volume: a PA-5220 at a busy OT perimeter generates 5,000-50,000 events/minute ≈ 7-72 GB/day. Plan storage accordingly. For long-term compliance evidence, use summary indexing to `audit_evidence` rather than retaining raw firewall logs for 7 years.
• NTP: firewalls must be synchronised within 1 second of the Splunk indexers. OT firewalls are often in isolated NTP domains — confirm with `show ntp` on PAN-OS or `show ntp status` on ASA.
• RBAC: create role `ot_network_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = firewall, ot_network, audit_evidence`. OT firewall logs reveal network architecture — restrict access.
• Baseline knowledge: Purdue model (IEC 62443 / ISA-95) defines hierarchical zones: Level 0 (sensors/actuators), Level 1 (PLCs/RTUs), Level 2 (HMIs/SCADA servers), Level 3 (OT DMZ / site operations), Level 3.5 (IT-OT DMZ), Level 4-5 (enterprise IT). The fundamental segmentation principle: no direct traffic from Level 4/5 (CORP-LAN) to Level 0/1/2 (PLC cells) without traversing the DMZ. This UC validates that principle by checking firewall logs against the policy CSV.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Palo Alto syslog to Splunk:**
On the PA firewall, configure a syslog server profile: Device > Server Profiles > Syslog > Add. Set the IP to the SC4S/Heavy Forwarder, port `6514` (TLS) or `514` (UDP), facility `LOG_USER`, format `BSD`. Assign this profile to all security-policy rules via Policies > Security > each rule > Actions > Log Setting > Log Forwarding Profile. Commit.

On the HF/SC4S, confirm events arrive: `index=firewall sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-5m | stats count`. Expect > 0 within 1 minute of the first traffic.

Key fields verification: `index=firewall sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-1h | stats dc(src_zone) as zones, dc(dest_zone) as dest_zones, dc(app) as apps | table zones, dest_zones, apps`. `zones` should be >= 3 (at minimum: CORP-LAN, OT-DMZ, PLC-CELL). If `zones=1` or `zones=0`, zone logging is not enabled or all rules use `any` zone — reconfigure the firewall policies.

**Cisco ASA syslog to Splunk:**
On the ASA, enable logging: `logging enable`, `logging trap informational`, `logging host <interface> <HF-IP>`. The TA classifies ASA syslog as `sourcetype=cisco:asa`. Zone mapping: by default, ASA uses interface names. Create meaningful interface names during ASA configuration (`nameif OT-CELL-1`, `nameif CORP-LAN`, `nameif SIS-ISOLATED`) so the zone names in Splunk are readable.

Verify: `index=firewall sourcetype=cisco:asa earliest=-1h | stats dc(src_zone) as zones`. If `src_zone` is null, the field alias in the Cisco ASA TA is not mapping `src_interface` to `src_zone` — edit `TA-cisco-asa/local/props.conf` to add `FIELDALIAS-zone_src = src_interface AS src_zone`.

**Zone naming normalisation across vendors:**
If both Palo Alto and Cisco firewalls are deployed, ensure consistent zone naming. The Palo Alto may call the corporate network `untrust` while the ASA calls it `outside`. Create a normalisation lookup `ot_zone_normalize.csv` with `raw_zone`, `normalized_zone` and apply via `| lookup ot_zone_normalize.csv src_zone AS raw_zone OUTPUT normalized_zone AS src_zone` in the search. Better: standardise zone names at the firewall configuration level during the next change window.

**Policy CSV deployment:**
Import `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` to `etc/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/` via Deployment Server. Validate: `| inputlookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv | stats count by allowed | table allowed count`. The ratio should reflect your architecture — typically 10-30 allowed pairs and 50-200 blocked pairs for a medium-complexity OT network.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.36 NIS2 OT Segmentation Validation`.

```spl
index=firewall sourcetype=pan:traffic OR sourcetype=cisco:asa earliest=-24h src_zone=* dest_zone=*
| lookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT allowed protocol port
| where allowed="false"
| stats sum(bytes) as bytes count as sessions by src_zone dest_zone dest_port app
| where sessions > 10
| sort - bytes
| table src_zone, dest_zone, dest_port, app, bytes, sessions
```

Understanding this SPL — design decisions that survive review:

• **24-hour lookback**: OT network anomalies should be detected within one shift cycle. Unlike the 7-day window used for Modbus protocol anomalies (UC-22.2.39), zone violations are immediately actionable — they indicate traffic flowing where it should not, right now.

• **`src_zone=* dest_zone=*` filter**: drops events where zone extraction failed (both fields null). Without this filter, events with missing zones would be looked up against the CSV and silently match on null — producing false positives.

• **`allowed="false"` filtering after lookup**: the lookup attaches the policy decision to each session. Only sessions crossing zone pairs explicitly marked as forbidden are retained. Zone pairs NOT in the CSV at all (unknown pairs) are silently dropped — this is intentional. Add `| fillnull allowed value="UNCLASSIFIED"` and `| where allowed="false" OR allowed="UNCLASSIFIED"` if you want to also flag unclassified zone pairs.

• **`sessions > 10` threshold**: a single stray packet crossing a forbidden zone pair is noise — it could be a routing flap, a broadcast leak, or a misconfigured NAT rule. Ten sessions in 24 hours is a sustained pattern. For high-security environments (SIS zones), tighten to `sessions > 1`. The threshold is deliberately set at the operator level, not hardcoded.

• **`sort - bytes`**: the zone pair moving the most data is the highest-priority investigation target. A pair with 10 sessions and 10 GB of bytes is far more concerning than one with 10 sessions and 1 KB — the former could be data exfiltration or bulk file transfer, the latter is likely control-plane chatter.

• **Both `pan:traffic` and `cisco:asa` in one search**: normalises across vendors. The TAs ensure both sourcetypes extract `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `bytes`, `dest_port` into the same field names. If field names differ between TA versions, add `| eval src_zone=coalesce(src_zone, src_interface)` as a fallback.

Schedule: cron `0 * * * *` (hourly). Time range: `-24h to now`. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`. Throttle by `src_zone + dest_zone` pair for 12 hours — a sustained violation should not re-page every hour during investigation.

CIM-accelerated variant (for high-volume environments):
```spl
| tstats summariesonly=true sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as bytes count as sessions from datamodel=Network_Traffic where All_Traffic.src_zone=* All_Traffic.dest_zone=* by All_Traffic.src_zone All_Traffic.dest_zone All_Traffic.dest_port All_Traffic.app
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT allowed
| where allowed="false" AND sessions > 10
| sort - bytes
```
This is 10-20x faster on large firewall deployments because the Network Traffic data model pre-aggregates the session data.

Alert action: (1) Create ES notable with `urgency=critical`, routed to OT SOC queue. Include `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `dest_port`, and `bytes` in the notable fields. (2) Page OT network engineer via PagerDuty. (3) If ES adaptive response is configured, auto-create a ServiceNow incident with attachment of the violation details. (4) Write to `index=audit_evidence`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Baseline zone count**: `index=firewall sourcetype=pan:traffic OR sourcetype=cisco:asa earliest=-24h | stats dc(src_zone) as src_zones, dc(dest_zone) as dest_zones`. Compare to the number of zones configured on your firewalls. If Splunk sees fewer zones than configured, some zone traffic is not being logged — check firewall log-forwarding profiles.

(b) **Policy CSV completeness**: `index=firewall sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-24h | stats count by src_zone dest_zone | lookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT allowed | where isnull(allowed) | sort - count`. This shows zone pairs that exist in traffic but are NOT in the policy CSV. Every pair should be classified. Unclassified pairs are blind spots.

(c) **Positive test — controlled RDP attempt**: from a CORP-LAN workstation, attempt an RDP connection (`mstsc.exe`) to an HMI IP address in a PLC cell zone. The firewall should log the session (even if blocked by the firewall rule). Run the search and confirm a row appears with `src_zone=CORP-LAN, dest_zone=PLC-CELL-1, dest_port=3389, app=ms-rdp`.

(d) **Negative test — approved maintenance pair**: during a planned maintenance window, add a temporary row to `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv` with `src_zone=JUMP-HOST, dest_zone=PLC-CELL-1, allowed=true, port=502, justification="Scheduled maintenance CHG-12345"`. Run the search. Confirm the JUMP-HOST → PLC-CELL-1 pair does NOT appear in the output.

(e) **Firewall session count parity**: pick a zone pair from the search results. On the Palo Alto: `show session all filter from-zone CORP-LAN to-zone PLC-CELL-1`. Compare the session count to Splunk's `sessions` value. They should be within 10% — differences indicate log forwarding latency or dropped syslog packets.

(f) **Cross-vendor consistency**: if both Palo Alto and ASA firewalls are in the path, run `index=firewall earliest=-24h src_zone="CORP-LAN" dest_zone="PLC-CELL-1" | stats count by sourcetype`. Both sourcetypes should show events for the same zone pair. If only one shows events, the other firewall is not logging zone transitions — check log-forwarding config.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 OT Segmentation Posture`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Forbidden zone crossings (24h)` (red >= 1). Single-value `Distinct forbidden paths active` (count of unique src_zone→dest_zone pairs). Single-value `Unclassified zone pairs` (from the completeness check in Step 3b — amber > 0).
• **Row 2 — Sankey diagram**: source zones on the left, destination zones on the right, edge thickness weighted by `bytes`. Forbidden crossings in red, allowed in green. This gives the OT governance team an immediate visual of traffic flow violations.
• **Row 3 — Violation table**: sortable by src_zone, dest_zone, dest_port, app, bytes, sessions. Drilldown opens raw firewall events for the selected zone pair (filtered to the last 4 hours for incident investigation).
• **Row 4 — Hourly heatmap**: sessions by hour-of-day and zone pair. Clusters at specific hours may indicate scheduled processes (false positive) or attacker activity patterns.
• **Row 5 — 30-day trend**: timechart from `index=audit_evidence` showing forbidden-crossing count over 30 days. Rising trend = segmentation is degrading. Flat zero = segmentation is holding.

Runbook (owner: OT Network Engineer):
1. Hourly: review the dashboard when paged, or at shift handover.
2. For `dest_port=3389` (RDP) or `dest_port=445` (SMB) crossings: these are the most dangerous — they indicate remote-desktop or file-sharing traffic from IT to OT. Investigate immediately: who initiated the session? Was it from a jump host or an unauthorized workstation?
3. For `dest_port=502` (Modbus) or `dest_port=102` (S7comm) crossings from CORP-LAN: this means IT-layer traffic is reaching PLC-layer protocols. Even if the firewall blocks it (`action=deny`), the attempt itself is a finding. Investigate the source IP — it may be a compromised IT workstation scanning for OT targets.
4. For `app=unknown-tcp` with high `bytes`: bulk data transfer across a forbidden zone pair. Could be data exfiltration (process data leaving the OT network) or unauthorized backup traffic.
5. After investigation: update the policy CSV if the traffic is legitimate (with justification and CHG ticket reference). If the traffic is illegitimate, escalate to OT incident response.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`src_zone` or `dest_zone` is null on firewall events.** The firewall is not logging zone information. For Palo Alto: check that security rules reference named zones, not `any`. Rules with `any` zone produce events with `src_zone=any` which is technically non-null but useless for segmentation validation — update the rules to use explicit zone names. For Cisco ASA: zone information comes from interface names — check that `nameif` is set on each interface (`show interface ip brief | include nameif`). If the TA is not mapping `src_interface` to `src_zone`, add the FIELDALIAS in `props.conf`.

• **Lookup returns `allowed=null` for most traffic.** The zone names in the policy CSV do not match the zone names in firewall events. Common mismatch: CSV uses Purdue-style names (`Level-0-SIS`) but the firewall uses short names (`SIS`). Fix: run `index=firewall earliest=-1h | stats dc(src_zone) | table src_zone` to get the actual zone names used in events, and update the CSV to match.

• **Huge `bytes` values on pairs that should be blocked.** The firewall is logging both `allow` and `deny` sessions for the same rule, and the denied sessions still show bytes (the bytes of the denied TCP SYN packets). Fix: add `| where action="allowed" OR action="allow"` to the search if you only want to see successful crossings. Remove this filter if you also want to see denied attempts (which are still valuable for threat detection).

• **Alert fires every night during backup window.** The backup server in the IT network backs up OT historian data across a forbidden zone pair. This is a legitimate operational requirement but a segmentation policy exception. Fix: add a row to the policy CSV with `allowed=true, protocol=tcp, port=445, justification="Nightly historian backup CHG-54321"`. Better: redesign the backup architecture to pull historian data from the OT DMZ (Level 3.5) instead of reaching into the OT cell zone.

• **Duplicate events from redundant firewalls.** Active-passive HA firewall pairs both forward syslog, but only one is active. The passive firewall may log state-sync traffic that looks like zone crossings. Fix: filter by `serial_number` or `fw_cluster_member` in the search to include only the active node. For Palo Alto: `| where serial_number="<active-serial>"`. For ASA: `| where failover_state="active"`.

• **No events for a zone pair that definitely has traffic.** The firewall rule for that zone pair does not have log forwarding enabled. This is common in Palo Alto deployments where the default "intrazone-default" rule logs at `session-end` but custom rules may have logging disabled. Fix: on PAN-OS, check each rule: Policies > Security > [rule] > Actions > Log at Session End. Enable logging and commit.

• **CIM-accelerated variant returns different results than raw.** The Network Traffic data model may not have the latest events accelerated yet. Check DMA status: `| rest /services/admin/summarization by_tstats=t | search datamodel_name=Network_Traffic | table datamodel_name eai:acl.app is_inprogress latest_time`. If `latest_time` is more than 1 hour behind, acceleration is lagging — either due to indexer load or because the data model definition was recently changed. Allow time for catch-up, or use the raw variant during the gap.
grandmaExplanation: Factories and plants keep fragile equipment on separate network lanes from the regular office Wi‑Fi. We watch the checkpoints between those lanes—the firewalls—and loudly report traffic patterns that behave like someone drove the wrong way through a one-way gate, especially when it keeps happening.
